points: The economy comprises three sectors classified by nature of activity: primary (agriculture, mining), secondary (manufacturing, industries), and tertiary (services). These sectors are highly interdependent—disruption in one affects others. Over 40 years (1977-2017), India's sectoral contribution to GVA shifted dramatically: tertiary rose to 55%, primary fell to 17%, while employment remained heavily in agriculture (50%), revealing a critical production-employment gap.

India faces severe underemployment, particularly disguised unemployment in agriculture where surplus workers share limited work. While agricultural output increased only 4 times in 40 years, agricultural employment remains at 50% of workforce, producing only 17% of GVA. Mechanization in secondary and tertiary sectors means fewer workers are needed per unit of production, creating insufficient job creation despite economic growth.

The unorganized sector (80% of workers) offers low wages, no job security, and no benefits, with vulnerable groups—landless labourers, small farmers, casual workers, and backward communities—facing exploitation. Government must provide essential services (education, healthcare, infrastructure, utilities) unprofitable for private sector. MGNREGA 2005 guarantees 100 days of rural employment, while long-term strategies require agricultural development, value addition industries, credit provision, and employment protection for unorganised workers.
